Event: Falls for repeated 419 scams and sends money
Explanation: Loses money to scams while giving it to fraudsters who don’t appear as real “others” in story terms.

Event: Barrels forward with crushes to borderline stalking
Explanation: Damages his own chances and makes others uncomfortable without any benefit.

Event: Tries to kiss Pam because they’re both lonely
Explanation: Embarrasses himself and deeply discomforts Pam with no gain.

Event: Chooses “Little Kid Lover” as online username
Explanation: Badly damages his own prospects and creeps others out for no benefit.

Event: Stages a fake suicide attempt to educate office
Explanation: Seriously endangers himself and traumatizes coworkers while providing almost no useful education.

Event: Actually risks death by using ineffective cushion
Explanation: Directly endangers his own life without affecting others in-story beyond concern.

Event: Achieves virtually untouchable sales record
Explanation: Gains strong career success while bringing in value for company and clients.

Event: Raises branch profits by 17% as manager
Explanation: Improves his standing and the branch’s financial performance substantially.

Event: Moves to Boulder to live with Holly after engagement
Explanation: Gets family life and long-term happiness while benefiting Holly via shared life.

Event: Supports Pam after her failed art gallery show
Explanation: Invests time/concern and emotionally helps Pam, modest self-benefit through bonding.

Event: Flies into petulant rage when feeling rejected
Explanation: Hurts relationships and office climate while also undermining his own respect.

Event: Launches long-game revenge on Dwight for job coup
Explanation: Protects his authority and self-respect at the cost of Dwight’s severe humiliation.

Event: Eats entire family-size pot pie and overloaded pretzel
Explanation: Indulges in unhealthy overeating that harms him with no benefit to others.

Event: Cannot keep secrets and leaks sensitive info
Explanation: Damages trust and sometimes harms others while also hurting his own reputation.

Event: Keeps Dwight–Angela affair from Andy for a while
Explanation: Manages information to delay hurt, then reveals privately, balancing harms and duties.

Event: Explodes at criticism like roast or prison joke
Explanation: Damages his authority and staff relations without gaining anything.

Event: Creates “Date Mike” persona and ruins a good date
Explanation: Tanks a promising connection and makes the woman uncomfortable for no gain.

Event: Goes overboard repeating any joke that once worked
Explanation: Brief self-satisfaction but erodes others’ patience and his social standing.

Event: Abuses “Chandler’s Law” by always pulling a gun in improv
Explanation: Undermines his own learning and ruins others’ scenes.

Event: Gets closure with past girlfriends and re-evaluates Ryan & Packer
Explanation: Gains emotional growth and better boundaries, mildly improves others’ situations.

Event: Puts Ryan and Jim in place asserting “It’s his office”
Explanation: Reasserts necessary authority in a mostly appropriate, effective way.

Event: Deprives cocky sales staff of rewards and gives leads to others
Explanation: Reclaims power and corrects arrogance but also unfairly harms top sellers’ income.

Event: Tricks Jim and Pam into accepting dinner invitation
Explanation: Gets desired social time at expense of their autonomy and comfort.

Event: Runs out first in office fires without checking on staff
Explanation: Protects himself while disregarding others’ safety.

Event: Lets Dwight take blame for failing Golden Ticket idea
Explanation: Shifts potential corporate punishment onto Dwight for his own protection.

Event: Acts as if mild burn is worse than Dwight’s concussion
Explanation: Seeks sympathy and disrupts work with exaggerated self-pity, harming respect and morale.

Event: Demands credit with no blame for Golden Ticket success
Explanation: Seeks status at the expense of fairness and coworkers’ recognition.

Event: Gives Jim “engaged ain’t married” relationship advice
Explanation: Provides blunt but useful advice that helps Jim pursue true relationship desires.

Event: Calls out Andy for hiding prior engagement from Erin
Explanation: Correctly frames Andy’s omission as serious, supporting Erin’s right to know.

Event: Realizes Darryl is using Roy attack as pay-raise leverage
Explanation: Correctly reads negotiation dynamics, modestly benefiting both sides in stable outcome.

Event: Refuses customer’s demand for resignation in Product Recall
Explanation: Protects his job while offering fair remedy to customer within reason.

Event: Rejects Dwight’s “bad advice” as sign of bad leadership
Explanation: Identifies that leaders are responsible for whom they follow, improving his own leadership insight.

Event: Argues Meredith was drunk when he hit her (deleted scene)
Explanation: Attempts to dodge responsibility by blaming victim, slightly shielding himself.

Event: Tells Stanley he must still treat him with boss-level respect
Explanation: Affirms necessary workplace hierarchy while accepting Stanley’s personal dislike.

Event: Negotiates Michael Scott Paper Co. buyout with David Wallace
Explanation: Secures jobs and favorable terms for himself and team while solving a corporate problem.

Event: Questions Jim & Pam for not telling him about pregnancy
Explanation: Gains some managerial justification but mostly makes it about himself, stressing them.

Event: Correctly suspects Donna wants more and is cheating
Explanation: Reads situation accurately, helping him make more informed relationship decisions.

Event: Refuses to send Oscar to jail by framing him for drugs
Explanation: Stops an unjust scheme, accepting loss of his petty revenge against Toby.

Event: Dismisses Jim’s brothers’ crude jokes before the wedding
Explanation: Protects the tone of Jim’s wedding and shows appropriate boundaries.

Event: Spanks and then fires Luke in front of the office
Explanation: Firing addresses problem, but public spanking is unprofessional and harms Michael more.

Event: Generally brushes off rules and infractions to be liked
Explanation: Loss of authority and structure without yielding stable friendship or performance gains.

Event: Treats employees as “babies” needing guidance
Explanation: Undercuts adult autonomy and his own credibility while gaining only a feeling of importance.

Event: Disciplines Stanley for open disrespect in “Did I Stutter”
Explanation: Finally enforces boundaries, improving respect and workplace order.

Event: Runs murder mystery game to keep morale up during crisis
Explanation: Uses play to manage panic; childish form but effective boost to morale in tough moment.

Event: Hires Kevin as accountant instead of warehouse worker
Explanation: Misjudges skills, saddling office with an incompetent accountant and future problems.

Event: Picks Stanley over Kevin for basketball
Explanation: Weakens his team and embarrasses Stanley while ignoring the actually skilled player.

Event: Insults Andy in front of class for badmouthing paper industry
Explanation: Uncharacteristically, correctly calls out Ryan’s disrespect and defends his field.

Event: Accepts he’ll never be friends with Stanley but demands respect
Explanation: Balances emotional reality with professional expectations, improving office dynamics.

Event: Becomes parental figure for Erin
Explanation: Gives Erin emotional support and guidance she lacks, also fulfilling his need to nurture.

Event: Publicly outs Stanley’s affair with Cynthia
Explanation: Reveals private matter in revenge, worsening Stanley’s life and eroding Michael’s trustworthiness.

Event: Spanks Luke in front of entire office for misbehavior
Explanation: Indulges anger via humiliating physical punishment that backfires on his image and career risk.

Event: Considers Todd Packer a friend and keeps enabling him
Explanation: Harms office culture and his own reputation by supporting an obvious jerk.

Event: Eventually rejects Todd Packer after going too far
Explanation: Cuts off a toxic “friend,” improving his environment and self-respect.

Event: Keeps bringing Ryan back despite repeated failures
Explanation: Invests in someone who hurts the company and himself, for misplaced affection.

Event: Foils Jim & Dwight’s plan to frame Toby when cops arrive
Explanation: Stops a cruel, potentially criminal scheme, protecting Toby and the office.

Event: Disciplines almost no one and cries when he must
Explanation: Weak leadership hurts both his authority and the office’s functioning.

Event: Invades Stanley’s privacy to discover his affair
Explanation: Needlessly violates privacy, gaining only gossip and damaging trust.

Event: Uses heavy machinery dangerously in the warehouse
Explanation: Risks serious harm to himself and others for jokes and thrills.

Event: Shows up to Pam’s art show when no one else does
Explanation: Provides emotional validation and support when she most needs it, deepening their bond.

Event: Tries to “play Cupid” at blood-drive singles event for own date
Explanation: Uses pretense of helping others mostly as a ploy for himself, with little success or benefit.

Event: Becomes Mean Boss after Jan breakup, blames Pam etc.
Explanation: Vents pain by mistreating staff, harming morale and respect without helping himself.

Event: Makes “Golden Ticket” idea that nearly costs company money
Explanation: Takes big marketing risk with little foresight, almost severely harming company finances.

Event: Takes second job to pay debts while Jan overspends
Explanation: Overworks himself due to poor financial decisions and partner’s spending, others unaffected.

Event: Makes many unnecessary purchases aiding his debt
Explanation: Directly harms his financial health through needless spending.

Event: Fires Tony after he tries to quit over Michael’s antics
Explanation: Tries to save face by firing him, costing company severance and worsening his own image.

Event: Drives car into lake to “prove” GPS is unreliable
Explanation: Endangers lives and property to make a nonsensical point; harms self and others.

Event: Tries to eat unidentified wild mushrooms
Explanation: Seriously risks poisoning himself for no clear benefit, others unaffected.

Event: Prepares to jump off roof onto bouncy castle in demo
Explanation: Again endangers his own life in front of coworkers with shaky safety measures.

Event: Takes kids to Chili’s, hosts Dundies, casino night etc.
Explanation: Improves camaraderie and morale, and gets affection/identity as “fun boss.”

Event: Tricks Todd Packer with fake Florida promotion (complicit)
Explanation: Escapes a toxic coworker with a cruel prank that significantly harms Packer.

Event: Makes every situation “all about him” in practice
Explanation: Undermines relationships and trust while never achieving real satisfaction.

Event: Uses Toby as a constant punching bag
Explanation: Gets fleeting catharsis while pointlessly hurting an inoffensive coworker and his own image.

Event: Steps up to protect employees when their wellbeing is at stake
Explanation: When cornered, reliably does the right thing, benefiting both himself and his staff.

Event: Runs branch in a way that becomes top-performing by S5
Explanation: Despite chaos, achieves outstanding results for himself and the team.

Event: Leaves Dunder Mifflin voluntarily rather than being fired
Explanation: Exits on his own terms for a happier life while avoiding an employment disaster.

Event: Becomes calmer, more mature family man by Dwight’s wedding
Explanation: Gains stable happiness and brings warmth and support to others.
